output.answer:
## Long-Running Issues Showing No Meaningful Progress: January–April 2026

Based on reasoning across the memos, two issues stand out as genuinely stuck across the full period. A third partial case is also worth flagging.

---

### 1. Nevada DMV Holds — Still Waiting on State Action

This is the clearest example of a stalled issue.

- **FEB-018** (January 2026 update) reports that Nevada DMV holds setup is "in progress" and that the software vendor AIMS is "awaiting Department of Public Safety approval."
- **APR-005** (February 2026 update, published April 14) reports the exact same situation: Nevada DMV holds "remain pending state-level action from the Nevada Department of Public Safety."

Between late January and mid-April, nothing changed. The City has no control over this — it is entirely dependent on a state agency acting — but the memos show zero movement across roughly three months. The City can collect from California-registered owners and Nevada owners who voluntarily pay, but the enforcement lever of placing holds on Nevada vehicle registrations remains unavailable. The memos suggest this gap meaningfully limits the parking program's ability to compel payment from local violators.

**What is stuck:** A state agency (Nevada Department of Public Safety) has not acted, and the City has no mechanism to force or accelerate that action.

---

### 2. Parking Meter Hardware Repairs — Deadline Slipped at Least Once

- **FEB-018** states that IPS Group technicians are replacing solar panels and batteries on single-space meters, with "full resolution targeted by end of February 2026."
- **APR-005** states the same work is "ongoing" and that "all critical issues [are] expected resolved by end of March 2026."

The deadline moved from end of February to end of March, and the April memo — covering February program data — still describes the work as ongoing without confirming completion. The memos do not include a follow-up confirming the March deadline was met.

**What is stuck:** A hardware maintenance task that was supposed to be finished in February was still unresolved as of the April memo's reporting window, with the goalposts having shifted at least once.

---

### 3. Partial Case: Homeless Outreach — Structural Throughput Remains Low

This is a softer pattern, but worth noting. **FEB-009** reports that in December 2025, the Clean and Safe Team conducted 69 outreach interactions but placed only 9 individuals into transitional housing — a roughly 13% conversion rate — against a backdrop of 127 arrests and 270 community service requests. The memo notes specific individuals still blocked from housing by documentation issues (missing IDs, inaccessible units).

No later memo in this set provides an updated Clean and Safe report for January through April, so a direct month-over-month comparison cannot be made. However, the December data alone illustrates a persistent structural gap: enforcement activity is high, but the pathway from outreach to stable housing placement is very narrow. The memos do not show any new resources, policy changes, or program expansions that would address this bottleneck.

**What is stuck:** The ratio of enforcement actions to actual housing placements, with no evidence in these memos of a structural fix being introduced.

---

### What Is NOT Stuck (for contrast)

The Virginia Range horse fencing project (FEB-010) had been delayed by three bidding rounds but was actively moving toward a contract award — that is a case of slow progress, not stagnation. The intern program (FEB-005) and Park Partners pilot (MAR-010) are both new initiatives still within their launch phases, so "no progress" does not apply to them.

FEB-005.title: Washoe County School District Intern Program
FEB-005.tldr: The Community Engagement and Services Department launched a pilot high school internship program in fall 2025 in partnership with the Washoe County School District's Career Technical Education program. Four students contributed 240 hours across four City departments in the fall; eight students are contributing 480 hours across six departments in spring 2026. The program provides hands-on local government experience and academic credit at no direct cost to the City, though it requires an administrative investment. Staff will evaluate the program's long-term sustainability at the end of the academic year.
FEB-005.key_stats: Fall 2025: 4 students, 4 departments, ~240 total internship hours. Spring 2026: 8 students, 6 departments, 480 total internship hours projected.
FEB-005.key_decisions: No formal Council decision requested; memo is informational. Staff will evaluate program sustainability and possible continuity at the conclusion of the current academic year.
FEB-005.action_items: Community Engagement and Services Department staff to evaluate the pilot program—including available staff capacity—at the end of the 2025-2026 academic year to determine long-term sustainability and possible program continuity.

FEB-010.title: Virginia Range Horse Fencing Progress Update
FEB-010.tldr: This memo updates the Mayor and City Council on the Virginia Range Horse Protection Project. Phase 1 fencing from Mira Loma Drive to Kenneth Way was completed in May 2025 at a total cost of $565,383, leaving $346,828 to carry forward. Phase 2 (Kenneth Way to Toll Road) has faced repeated bidding challenges but a low bid of $686,335 from Soil Tech, Inc. was received on January 22, 2026, and staff will seek Council approval of a $702,354 contract (bid plus contingency) on February 25, 2026, fully funded at $789,328.
FEB-010.key_stats: Phase 1 total funding: $912,211; Phase 1 total expenditures: $565,383; Phase 1 carry-forward to Phase 2: $346,828. Phase 2 total funding: $789,328; Low bid (Soil Tech, Inc.): $686,335; Phase 2 contract amount with contingency: $702,354. Phase 2 bidding required three rounds: first bids received May 1, 2025 (not awarded due to easement issues); second bids opened December 2, 2025 (both non-responsive); third bids opened January 22, 2026 (two responsive bidders).
FEB-010.key_decisions: Council previously authorized the City Manager to award a Phase 2 contract not to exceed $660,000 (December 11, 2024). The low bid of $686,335 exceeds that authorization, so staff will bring a new agenda item on February 25, 2026, requesting Council authorize the bid award plus contingency totaling $702,354.
FEB-010.action_items: Staff to place item on February 25, 2026 Council agenda to authorize award of Phase 2 construction contract to Soil Tech, Inc. for $686,335 plus $16,019 contingency, totaling $702,354.

APR-002.title: 04.06.26 Weekly Reports Public Records Request, Media Requests, and Code Enforcement Services Requests
APR-002.tldr: This memo transmits weekly activity reports from the City Clerk's Office, Strategic Communications, and Code Enforcement to the Mayor and City Council for the period of March 27 through April 2, 2026. The bulk of the attached data consists of public records requests logged by the City Clerk's Office, covering police reports, body-worn camera video, building records, 911 logs, fire prevention records, and code enforcement inquiries. The memo was prepared by Management Assistant Esha Singh and routed through City Manager Jackie Bryant. No policy decisions or budget items are presented; the memo is purely informational.
APR-002.key_stats: Partial weekly report data shown covers at least 63 public records requests logged between March 27–28, 2026, spanning Police Department Records, General Public Records, and Fire Department Records categories.
APR-002.action_items: Mayor and City Council to review attached weekly reports: (A) Public records requests from City Clerk's Office, (B) Media requests from Strategic Communications, (C) Code enforcement service requests from Code Enforcement.

MAR-007.title: 03.13.26 Weekly Reports Public Records Request, Media Requests, and Code Enforcement Services Requests
MAR-007.tldr: This memo transmits weekly activity reports to the Mayor and City Council for the period of March 6–12, 2026. It covers public records requests received by the City Clerk's Office, media requests handled by Strategic Communications, and code enforcement service requests. The attached logs detail individual requestors, request types, and categories across General, Police, and Fire records.
MAR-007.key_stats: The partial log shown spans at least 65+ individual public records requests filed between March 6–7, 2026 alone, covering categories including Police Reports, Background Requests, Body Worn Camera Video, Fire Prevention Records, Building Plans/Permits, 911 Transcripts, Traffic Accidents, and Code Enforcement Violations. Multiple requests were submitted by recurring entities such as OPM (5 background requests on 3/6) and CRU LexisNexis (5 police reports on 3/6).
MAR-007.action_items: No action items specified; memo is informational. Attachments A (Public Records), B (Media Requests), and C (Code Enforcement Service Requests) are referenced as supporting documents.

APR-005.title: Parking Program Updates – February 2026
APR-005.tldr: The City of Reno's Parking Program update for February 2026 reports $633,339.85 in total parking fines and fees collected since DMV registered owner data access was reinstated on June 20, 2025. Adjudication activity in January and February 2026 reduced original violation balances by roughly 55–60%. Software vendor AIMS is pursuing California mail notifications and awaiting state action on Nevada DMV holds. IPS technicians are ongoing in replacing solar panels and batteries on single-space meters, with all critical issues expected resolved by end of March 2026.
APR-005.key_stats: Total collected since June 20, 2025: $633,339.85 across 18,156 tickets paid. February 2026: 1,206 tickets paid, $54,058.75 collected. January 2026 adjudications: 89 cases, original balance $8,914.00 reduced to $3,545.00. February 2026 adjudications: 119 cases, original balance $11,441.00 reduced to $5,813.00.
APR-005.key_decisions: Staff will continue monitoring revenue collections weekly and may send unpaid citations to collections. Staff will review AIMS quote for California mail notifications before proceeding. Nevada DMV holds remain pending state-level action from the Nevada Department of Public Safety.
APR-005.action_items: 1. Monitor parking revenue collections weekly. 2. Review AIMS quote for California registered owner mail notifications and determine next steps. 3. Await Nevada Department of Public Safety action to complete DMV holds setup. 4. Work with AIMS to develop expanded reports with additional metrics for future memos. 5. Ensure IPS resolves all battery-critical meter issues by end of March 2026.

MAR-010.title: Park Partners Pilot Program at Oxbow and Summit Ridge Park
MAR-010.tldr: The Parks and Recreation Department is launching the Park Partners pilot program at Oxbow Nature Study and Summit Ridge Park in April 2026. The program creates a structured way for community members to report safety concerns and maintenance issues via signage, phone numbers, and a QR code linked to Reno Direct. Active reporters may be invited to volunteer for hands-on park upkeep tasks. The pilot runs through December 2026, with quarterly updates to City Council and potential expansion to other parks if successful.
MAR-010.key_stats: Pilot runs April–December 2026 at 2 parks (Oxbow Nature Study and Summit Ridge Park); quarterly updates to City Council; potential future expansion to Teglia's Paradise Park and Virginia Lake Park.
MAR-010.key_decisions: Approval/awareness of the Park Partners pilot program launch at Oxbow Nature Study and Summit Ridge Park; program evaluation criteria include quantity of service requests reported and number of dedicated volunteers.
MAR-010.action_items: Install signs at Oxbow Nature Study and Summit Ridge Park in March 2026; hold media event with Communications Department in early April 2026; track reports via Reno Direct; provide quarterly updates to City Council; evaluate program in December 2026; expand to additional parks if successful.

MAR-020.title: CORRECTED MEMO 03.23.26 Weekly Reports Public Records Request, Media Requests, and Code Enforcement Services Requests
MAR-020.tldr: This memo from the City Manager's Office transmits weekly reports to the Mayor and City Council covering March 13–19, 2026. The reports include public records requests received by the City Clerk's Office, media requests handled by Strategic Communications, and code enforcement service requests. The attached logs detail individual requests by reference number, date, requester, and category.
MAR-020.key_stats: The public records log (run date 03/20/2026) lists dozens of requests submitted on 3/13/2026 and 3/14/2026, spanning Police Department Records (police reports, background requests, body worn camera video, traffic accidents, FBI NICS), Fire Department Records (investigation reports, fire prevention records), and General Public Records (building plans/permits, 911 dispatch logs, sewer/GIS mapping, business licenses, contracts/ordinances, environmental assessments, capital projects).
MAR-020.action_items: Mayor and City Council to review attached weekly reports: (A) Public Records Requests from City Clerk's Office, (B) Media Requests from Strategic Communications, (C) Code Enforcement Service Requests from Code Enforcement.

FEB-018.title: Parking Program Updates - January 2026
FEB-018.tldr: This memo updates the Mayor and Council on the City of Reno Parking Program as of January 2026. Since regaining access to Nevada DMV registered owner data on June 20, 2025, the City has collected $579,281.10 in parking fines and fees. New access to California DMV data has been established, Nevada DMV holds setup is in progress, and IPS Group technicians are actively replacing solar panels and batteries on single-space meters with resolution expected by end of February 2026.
FEB-018.key_stats: 15,655 tickets paid ($530,534.89) from June 20–Dec 31, 2025; 1,295 tickets paid ($48,746.21) in January 2026; total collected: $579,281.10. January 2026 adjudications: 89 cases, original violation balance $8,914.00 reduced to $3,545.00 after adjudication.
FEB-018.key_decisions: Council previously approved (November 19, 2025) an agreement with IPS Group for gateway merchant fees and meter maintenance parts for one year with up to four renewal options, not to exceed $200,000.
FEB-018.action_items: Staff to monitor revenue collections weekly and send unpaid citations to collections as needed; AIMS awaiting Department of Public Safety approval for Nevada DMV holds; staff working with AIMS on expanded reporting metrics; IPS Group technicians continuing meter battery/solar panel replacements with full resolution targeted by end of February 2026.

APR-004.title: 04.14.26 Weekly Reports Public Records Request, Media Requests, and Code Enforcement Services Requests
APR-004.tldr: This memorandum transmits weekly reports to the Mayor and City Council covering April 3–9, 2026, from three city offices. The reports include public records requests received by the City Clerk's Office, media requests handled by Strategic Communications, and code enforcement service requests. The bulk of the attached detail visible in the memo consists of public records requests, predominantly for police department records such as police reports, body worn camera video, background requests, and 911 dispatch logs.
APR-004.key_stats: Public records requests logged from April 3–5, 2026 alone span at least pages 1–3 of 13, with the majority being Police Department Records requests. Frequent requestors include CRU LexisNexis (multiple requests per day), WCHSA CPS, OPM, and FIREARMSHELP@DPS.STATE.NV.US. Request types include Police Reports, Body Worn Camera Video, Background Requests, 911/Dispatch Calls, Fire Incident/Investigation Reports, and Building Plans/Permits.

FEB-008.title: 02.11.26 Weekly Reports Public Records Requests, Media Requests, and Code Enforcement Service Requests
FEB-008.tldr: This memo transmits weekly reports to the Mayor and City Council covering the period of January 30 through February 5, 2026. The reports include public records requests received by the City Clerk's Office, media requests handled by Strategic Communications, and code enforcement service requests. The attached logs detail individual requests by reference number, date, requester, and category.
FEB-008.key_stats: The visible portion of the public records log (pages 1–3 of 15) shows at least 21 Police Department Records requests, at least 15 Fire Department Records requests (all from CRU LexisNexis on 2/1/2026), and at least 14 General Public Records requests submitted between January 30 and February 1, 2026. Common request categories include Police Reports, Background Requests, FBI NICS, Body Worn Camera Video, Traffic Accidents, 911 Transcripts/Logs, Building Plans/Permits, and Fire Incident Reports.

FEB-009.title: December 2025 Clean and Safe Team Homeless Actions and Outcomes
FEB-009.tldr: The Clean and Safe Team's December 2025 report summarizes homeless outreach, enforcement, and maintenance activities across Reno. Key outcomes include 69 outreach interactions, 9 individuals placed into transitional housing, 127 arrests, and 30 encampment clean-ups. Community service requests totaled 270, a 9% decrease from November, with occupied vehicle nuisance comprising nearly half of all requests. Multiple city departments and contracted security coordinated efforts citywide.
FEB-009.key_stats: 69 outreach interactions; 9 individuals into transitional housing; 2 shelter arrangements; 1 ID replacement; 26 resources accessed; 201 citations (187 RPD + 14 Park/River Rangers); 127 arrests (31 felony, 96 misdemeanor); 3 large encampment clean-ups; 27 smaller encampment clean-ups; 182 yards of trash collected; 42 occupied vehicles posted; 5 occupied vehicles towed; 4 park exclusions; 40 code enforcement cases; 174 Allied Security site visits; 605 Park and River Ranger site visits; 270 total Reno Direct service requests (9% decrease from November)
FEB-009.action_items: Couple at Motel 6 to provide updated award letter to Kaytlin Canner; two individuals at Wells Footbridge need ID and Social Security card obtained for Permanent Supportive Housing program eligibility; couple diverted to motel assistance program after Cheryl Apartments unit was inaccessible due to stairs

FEB-020.title: 02.27.26 Weekly Reports Public Records Request, Media Requests, and Code Enforcement Services Requests
FEB-020.tldr: This memo transmits weekly reports to the Mayor and City Council covering the period of February 20–26, 2026. The reports include public records requests received by the City Clerk's Office, media requests handled by Strategic Communications, and code enforcement service requests. The attached logs detail dozens of individual requests spanning police records, fire prevention records, building permits, 911 dispatch logs, and other general public records categories.
FEB-020.key_stats: The visible portion of the public records log (pages 1–2 of 13) shows at least 57 requests logged between February 20–22, 2026, spanning Police Department Records, General Public Records, and Fire Department Records categories. The most common request types include Police Reports, Body Worn Camera Video, Building Plans/Permits/Inspections, and 911/Dispatch Calls.
FEB-020.action_items: No action items required of the Mayor or City Council; memo is informational only. Attached reports (Attachments A, B, and C) are provided for awareness.
